From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [Pixels](/w/index.php?title=Pixels&redirect=no "Pixels"))

For other uses, see [Pixel (disambiguation)](/wiki/Pixel_(disambiguation) "Pixel (disambiguation)").

Physical point in a raster image

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/2/2b/Pixel-example.png)](/wiki/File:Pixel-example.png)

This example shows an image with a portion greatly enlarged so that individual pixels, rendered as small squares, can easily be seen.

In [digital imaging](/wiki/Digital_imaging "Digital imaging"), a **pixel** (abbreviated **px**), **pel**,[[1]](#cite_note-1) or **picture element**[[2]](#cite_note-2) is the smallest addressable physical element of a [raster image](/wiki/Raster_image "Raster image") or the smallest controllable element of a [display device](/wiki/Display_device "Display device") or [dot matrix](/wiki/Dot_matrix "Dot matrix") printer. Pixels are arranged in a regular, two-dimensional grid, and each pixel serves as a sample of an original image, with a greater number of samples typically providing more accurate representations. Each pixel possesses a specific intensity or color, often composed of three or four component intensities, such as red, green, and blue (RGB), or cyan, magenta, yellow, and black (CMYK). The intensity of each pixel is variable, and in color imaging systems, these components are combined to produce a wide spectrum of colors. The concept of a picture element has existed since the early days of television, appearing as "Bildpunkt" in a 1888 German patent, and the term "pixel" has been used in various U.S. patents since 1911. In most digital [display devices](/wiki/Display_device "Display device"), pixels are the smallest element that can be manipulated through software.

Each pixel is a [sample](/wiki/Sampling_(signal_processing) "Sampling (signal processing)") of an original image; more samples typically provide more accurate representations of the original. The [intensity](/wiki/Intensity_(physics) "Intensity (physics)") of each pixel is variable. In color imaging systems, a color is typically represented by three or four component intensities such as [red, green, and blue](/wiki/RGB_color_model "RGB color model"), or [cyan, magenta, yellow, and black](/wiki/CMYK_color_model "CMYK color model").

In some contexts (such as descriptions of [camera sensors](/wiki/Camera_sensor "Camera sensor")), *pixel* refers to a single scalar element of a multi-component representation (called a *photosite* in the camera sensor context, although *[sensel](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/sensel "wikt:sensel")* 'sensor element' is sometimes used),[[3]](#cite_note-3) while in yet other contexts (like MRI) it may refer to a set of component intensities for a spatial position.

[Software](/wiki/Software "Software") on early consumer computers was necessarily rendered at a low resolution, with large pixels visible to the naked eye; graphics made under these limitations may be called [pixel art](/wiki/Pixel_art "Pixel art"), especially in reference to video games. Modern computers and displays, however, can easily render orders of magnitude more pixels than was previously possible, necessitating the use of large measurements like the [megapixel](#Megapixel) (one million pixels).

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pixel&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/de/Closeup_of_pixels.JPG/250px-Closeup_of_pixels.JPG)](/wiki/File:Closeup_of_pixels.JPG)

A photograph of [subpixel](/wiki/Subpixel_rendering "Subpixel rendering") display elements on a laptop's [LCD](/wiki/Liquid-crystal_display "Liquid-crystal display") screen

The word *pixel* is a combination of *pix* (from "pictures", shortened to "pics") and *el* (for "*[element](/wiki/Data_element "Data element")*"); similar formations with '*el'* include the words *[voxel](/wiki/Voxel "Voxel")*[[4]](#cite_note-JF-4) 'volume pixel', and [*texel*](/wiki/Texel_(graphics) "Texel (graphics)") 'texture pixel'.[[4]](#cite_note-JF-4) The word *pix* appeared in *[Variety](/wiki/Variety_(magazine) "Variety (magazine)")* magazine headlines in 1932, as an abbreviation for the word *pictures*, in reference to movies.[[5]](#cite_note-onetdict-5) By 1938, "pix" was being used in reference to still pictures by photojournalists.[[6]](#cite_note-lyon-6)

The word "pixel" was first published in 1965 by [Frederic C. Billingsley](/wiki/Frederic_C._Billingsley "Frederic C. Billingsley") of [JPL](/wiki/Jet_Propulsion_Laboratory "Jet Propulsion Laboratory"), to describe the picture elements of scanned images from [space probes](/wiki/Space_probe "Space probe") to the Moon and Mars.[[7]](#cite_note-7) Billingsley had learned the word from Keith E. McFarland, at the Link Division of General Precision in [Palo Alto](/wiki/Palo_Alto,_California "Palo Alto, California"), who in turn said he did not know where it originated. McFarland said simply it was "in use at the time" (c. 1963).[[6]](#cite_note-lyon-6)

The concept of a "picture element" dates to the earliest days of television, for example as "*Bildpunkt*" (the German word for *pixel*, literally 'picture point') in the 1888 German patent of [Paul Nipkow](/wiki/Paul_Nipkow "Paul Nipkow"). According to various etymologies, the earliest publication of the term *picture element* itself was in *[Wireless World](/wiki/Wireless_World "Wireless World")* magazine in 1927,[[8]](#cite_note-8) though it had been used earlier in various U.S. patents filed as early as 1911.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

Some authors explain *pixel* as *picture cell,* as early as 1972.[[10]](#cite_note-10) In [graphics](/wiki/Graphics "Graphics") and in image and video processing, *pel* is often used instead of *pixel*.[[11]](#cite_note-11) For example, IBM used it in their Technical Reference for the [original PC](/wiki/IBM_Personal_Computer "IBM Personal Computer").

[Pixilation](/wiki/Pixilation "Pixilation"), spelled with a second *i*, is an unrelated filmmaking technique that dates to the beginnings of cinema, in which live actors are posed frame by frame and photographed to create stop-motion animation. An archaic British word meaning "possession by spirits ([pixies](/wiki/Pixie "Pixie"))", the term has been used to describe the animation process since the early 1950s; various animators, including [Norman McLaren](/wiki/Norman_McLaren "Norman McLaren") and [Grant Munro](/wiki/Grant_Munro_(filmmaker) "Grant Munro (filmmaker)"), are credited with popularizing it.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

## Technical

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pixel&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Technical")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/c/c4/ReconstructionsFromPixels.png)](/wiki/File:ReconstructionsFromPixels.png)

A pixel does not need to be rendered as a small square. This image shows alternative ways of reconstructing an image from a set of pixel values, using dots, lines, or smooth filtering.

A pixel is generally thought of as the smallest single component of a [digital image](/wiki/Digital_image "Digital image"). However, the definition is highly context-sensitive. For example, there can be "[printed pixels](/wiki/CMYK "CMYK")" in a page, or pixels carried by electronic signals, or represented by digital values, or pixels on a display device, or pixels in a [digital camera](/wiki/Digital_camera "Digital camera") (photosensor elements). This list is not exhaustive and, depending on context, synonyms include pel, sample, byte, bit, dot, and spot. *Pixels* can be used as a unit of measure such as: 2400 pixels per inch, 640 pixels per line, or spaced 10 pixels apart.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a9/FILE_Hipers%C3%B4nica_Rio_09_-_8_BIT_Game_People%2C_Festival_Internacional_de_Linguagem_Eletr%C3%B4nica.jpg/250px-FILE_Hipers%C3%B4nica_Rio_09_-_8_BIT_Game_People%2C_Festival_Internacional_de_Linguagem_Eletr%C3%B4nica.jpg)](/wiki/File:FILE_Hipers%C3%B4nica_Rio_09_-_8_BIT_Game_People,_Festival_Internacional_de_Linguagem_Eletr%C3%B4nica.jpg)

Pixel art

The measures "[dots per inch](/wiki/Dots_per_inch "Dots per inch")" (dpi) and "[pixels per inch](/wiki/Pixels_per_inch "Pixels per inch")" (ppi) are sometimes used interchangeably, but have distinct meanings, especially for printer devices, where dpi is a measure of the printer's density of dot (e.g. ink droplet) placement.[[13]](#cite_note-13) For example, a high-quality photographic image may be printed with 600 ppi on a 1200 dpi inkjet printer.[[14]](#cite_note-14) Even higher dpi numbers, such as the 4800 dpi quoted by printer manufacturers since 2002, do not mean much in terms of achievable [resolution](/wiki/Image_resolution "Image resolution").[[15]](#cite_note-15)

The more pixels used to represent an image, the closer the result can resemble the original. The number of pixels in an image is sometimes called the resolution, though resolution has a more specific definition. Pixel counts can be expressed as a single number, as in a "three-megapixel" digital camera, which has a nominal three million pixels, or as a pair of numbers, as in a "640 by 480 display", which has 640 pixels from side to side and 480 from top to bottom (as in a [VGA](/wiki/Video_Graphics_Array "Video Graphics Array") display) and therefore has a total number of 640 × 480 = 307,200 pixels, or 0.3 megapixels.

The pixels, or color samples, that form a digitized image (such as a [JPEG](/wiki/JPEG "JPEG") file used on a web page) may or may not be in one-to-one [correspondence](/wiki/Bijection "Bijection") with screen pixels, depending on how a computer displays an image. In computing, an image composed of pixels is known as a *[bitmapped image](/wiki/Bitmap "Bitmap")* or a *[raster image](/wiki/Raster_graphics "Raster graphics")*. The word *raster* originates from [television scanning](/wiki/Raster_scan "Raster scan") patterns, and has been widely used to describe similar [halftone](/wiki/Halftone "Halftone") printing and storage techniques.

### Sampling patterns

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pixel&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Sampling patterns")]

For convenience, pixels are normally arranged in a [regular two-dimensional grid](/wiki/Regular_grid "Regular grid"). By using this arrangement, many common operations can be implemented by uniformly applying the same operation to each pixel independently. Other arrangements of pixels are possible, with some sampling patterns even changing the shape (or [kernel](/wiki/Convolution_kernel "Convolution kernel")) of each pixel across the image. For this reason, care must be taken when acquiring an image on one device and displaying it on another, or when converting image data from one pixel format to another.

For example:

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/8/86/Wikipedia_ClearType.png)](/wiki/File:Wikipedia_ClearType.png)

Subpixel text rendering using [ClearType](/wiki/ClearType "ClearType")

* [Liquid-crystal displays](/wiki/Liquid-crystal_display "Liquid-crystal display") (LCDs) typically use a staggered grid, where the red, green, and blue components are sampled at slightly different locations. [Subpixel rendering](/wiki/Subpixel_rendering "Subpixel rendering") is a technology which takes advantage of these differences to improve the rendering of text on LCD screens.
* The vast majority of color digital cameras use a [Bayer filter](/wiki/Bayer_filter "Bayer filter"), resulting in a regular grid of pixels where the *color* of each pixel depends on its position on the grid.
* A [clipmap](/wiki/Clipmap "Clipmap") uses a hierarchical sampling pattern, where the size of the [support](/wiki/Support_(mathematics) "Support (mathematics)") of each pixel depends on its location within the hierarchy.
* Warped grids are used when the underlying geometry is non-planar, such as images of the earth from space.[[16]](#cite_note-16)
* The use of non-uniform grids is an active research area, attempting to bypass the traditional [Nyquist limit](/wiki/Nyquist_rate "Nyquist rate").[[17]](#cite_note-17)
* Pixels on computer monitors are normally "square" (that is, have equal horizontal and vertical sampling pitch); pixels in other systems are often "rectangular" (that is, have unequal horizontal and vertical sampling pitch – oblong in shape), as are [digital video](/wiki/Digital_video "Digital video") formats with diverse [aspect ratios](/wiki/Pixel_aspect_ratio "Pixel aspect ratio"), such as the [anamorphic widescreen](/wiki/Anamorphic_widescreen "Anamorphic widescreen") formats of the [Rec. 601](/wiki/Rec._601 "Rec. 601") digital video standard.

### Resolution of computer monitors

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pixel&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Resolution of computer monitors")]

Computer monitors (and TV sets) generally have a fixed [native resolution](/wiki/Native_resolution "Native resolution"). What it is depends on the monitor, and size. See below for historical exceptions.

Computers can use pixels to display an image, often an abstract image that represents a [GUI](/wiki/GUI "GUI"). The resolution of this image is called the display resolution and is determined by the [video card](/wiki/Video_card "Video card") of the computer. [Flat-panel monitors](/wiki/Flat-panel_monitor "Flat-panel monitor") (and TV sets), e.g. [OLED](/wiki/OLED "OLED") or [LCD](/wiki/LCD "LCD") monitors, or [E-ink](/wiki/Electronic_paper "Electronic paper"), also use pixels to display an image, and have a [native resolution](/wiki/Native_resolution "Native resolution"), and it should (ideally) be matched to the video card resolution. Each pixel is made up of [triads](/wiki/Triad_(monitors) "Triad (monitors)"), with the number of these triads determining the native resolution.

On older, historically available, [CRT](/wiki/Cathode-ray_tube "Cathode-ray tube") monitors the resolution was possibly adjustable (still lower than what modern monitor achieve), while on some such monitors (or TV sets) the beam sweep rate was fixed, resulting in a fixed native resolution. Most CRT monitors do not have a fixed beam sweep rate, meaning they do not have a native resolution at all – instead they have a set of resolutions that are equally well supported. To produce the sharpest images possible on a flat-panel, e.g. OLED or LCD, the user must ensure the display resolution of the computer matches the native resolution of the monitor.

### Resolution of telescopes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pixel&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Resolution of telescopes")]

The pixel scale used in [astronomy](/wiki/Astronomy "Astronomy") is the angular distance between two objects on the sky that fall one pixel apart on the detector (CCD or infrared chip). The scale *s* measured in [radians](/wiki/Radian "Radian") is the ratio of the pixel spacing *p* and [focal length](/wiki/Focal_length "Focal length") *f* of the preceding optics, *s* = *p / f*. (The focal length is the product of the [focal ratio](/wiki/F-number "F-number") by the diameter of the associated lens or mirror.)

Because *s* is usually expressed in units of [arcseconds](/wiki/Arcsecond#Symbols_and_abbreviations "Arcsecond") per pixel, because 1 radian equals (180/π) × 3600 ≈ 206,265 arcseconds, and because focal lengths are often given in millimeters and pixel sizes in micrometers which yields another factor of 1,000, the formula is often quoted as *s* = 206 *p / f*.

### Bits per pixel

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pixel&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Bits per pixel")]

Main article: [Color depth](/wiki/Color_depth "Color depth")

The number of distinct colors that can be represented by a pixel depends on the number of bits per pixel (bpp). A 1 bpp image uses 1 bit for each pixel, so each pixel can be either on or off. Each additional bit doubles the number of colors available, so a 2 bpp image can have 4 colors, and a 3 bpp image can have 8 colors:

* 1 bpp, 21 = 2 colors ([monochrome](/wiki/Binary_image "Binary image"))
* 2 bpp, 22 = 4 colors
* 3 bpp, 23 = 8 colors
* 4 bpp, 24 = 16 colors
* 8 bpp, 28 = 256 colors
* 16 bpp, 216 = 65,536 colors ("[Highcolor](/wiki/Highcolor "Highcolor")" )
* 24 bpp, 224 = 16,777,216 colors ("[Truecolor](/wiki/24-bit_color "24-bit color")")

For color depths of 15 or more bits per pixel, the depth is normally the sum of the bits allocated to each of the red, green, and blue components. [Highcolor](/wiki/Highcolor "Highcolor"), usually meaning 16 bpp, normally has five bits for red and blue each, and six bits for green, as the human eye is more sensitive to errors in green than in the other two primary colors. For applications involving transparency, the 16 bits may be divided into five bits each of red, green, and blue, with one bit left for transparency. A 24-bit depth allows 8 bits per component. On some systems, 32-bit depth is available: this means that each 24-bit pixel has an extra 8 bits to describe its [opacity](/wiki/Opacity_(optics) "Opacity (optics)") (for purposes of combining with another image).

### Subpixels

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pixel&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Subpixels")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4d/Pixel_geometry_01_Pengo.jpg/250px-Pixel_geometry_01_Pengo.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pixel_geometry_01_Pengo.jpg)

Geometry of color elements of various CRTs and LCDs

Many display and image-acquisition systems are not capable of displaying or sensing the different [color channels](/wiki/Channel_(digital_image) "Channel (digital image)") at the same site. Therefore, the pixel grid is divided into single-color regions that contribute to the displayed or sensed color when viewed at a distance. In some displays, such as LCD, LED, and plasma displays, these single-color regions are separately addressable elements, which have come to be known as **subpixels**, mostly [RGB](/wiki/RGB "RGB") colors.[[18]](#cite_note-18) For example, [LCDs](/wiki/LCD "LCD") typically divide each pixel vertically into three subpixels. When the square pixel is divided into three subpixels, each subpixel is necessarily rectangular. In display industry terminology, subpixels are often referred to as *pixels*, as they are the basic addressable elements in a viewpoint of hardware, and hence *pixel circuits* rather than *subpixel circuits* is used.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/75/Samsung_TV_subpixels..jpg/250px-Samsung_TV_subpixels..jpg)](/wiki/File:Samsung_TV_subpixels..jpg)

[Samsung](/wiki/Samsung "Samsung") UA40M5860AKCHD TV subpixels

Most digital camera [image sensors](/wiki/Image_sensor "Image sensor") use single-color sensor regions, for example using the [Bayer filter](/wiki/Bayer_filter "Bayer filter") pattern, and in the camera industry these are known as *pixels* just like in the display industry, not *subpixels*.

For systems with subpixels, two different approaches can be taken:

* The subpixels can be ignored, with full-color pixels being treated as the smallest addressable imaging element; or
* The subpixels can be included in rendering calculations, which requires more analysis and processing time, but can produce apparently superior images in some cases.

This latter approach, referred to as [subpixel rendering](/wiki/Subpixel_rendering "Subpixel rendering"), uses knowledge of [pixel geometry](/wiki/Pixel_geometry "Pixel geometry") to manipulate the three colored subpixels separately, producing an increase in the apparent resolution of color displays. While [CRT](/wiki/Cathode-ray_tube "Cathode-ray tube") displays use red-green-blue-masked phosphor areas, dictated by a mesh grid called the shadow mask, it would require a difficult calibration step to be aligned with the displayed pixel raster, and so CRTs do not use subpixel rendering.

The concept of subpixels is related to [samples](/wiki/Sample_(graphics) "Sample (graphics)").

### Logical pixel

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pixel&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Logical pixel")]

In graphic, web design, and user interfaces, a "pixel" may refer to a fixed length rather than a true pixel on the screen to accommodate different [pixel densities](/wiki/Pixel_densities "Pixel densities"). A typical definition, such as in [CSS](/wiki/CSS "CSS"), is that a "physical" pixel is 1⁄96 inch (0.26 mm). Doing so makes sure a given element will display as the same size no matter what screen resolution views it.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

There may, however, be some further adjustments between a "physical" pixel and an on-screen logical pixel. As screens are viewed at difference distances (consider a phone, a computer display, and a TV), the desired length (a "reference pixel") is scaled relative to a reference viewing distance (28 inches (71 cm) in CSS). In addition, as true screen pixel densities are rarely multiples of 96 dpi, some rounding is often applied so that a logical pixel is an integer amount of actual pixels. Doing so avoids render artifacts. The final "pixel" obtained after these two steps becomes the "anchor" to which all other absolute measurements (e.g. the "centimeter") are based on.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

Worked example, with a 30-inch (76 cm) 2160p TV placed 56 inches (140 cm) away from the viewer:

* Calculate the scaled pixel size as 1⁄96 in × (56/28) = 1⁄48 inch (0.53 mm).
* Calculate the DPI of the TV as 2160 / (30 in / √9^2 + 16^2 × 16) ≈ 82.61 dpi.
* Calculate the real-pixel count per logical-pixel as 1⁄48 in × 82.61 dpi ≈ 1.721 pixels.

A browser will then choose to use the 1.721× pixel size, or round to a 2× ratio.

## Megapixel

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pixel&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Megapixel")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f7/Sensoraufl%C3%B6sungen.svg/330px-Sensoraufl%C3%B6sungen.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Sensoraufl%C3%B6sungen.svg)

Diagram of common sensor resolutions of digital cameras including megapixel values

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4a/Megapixel_comparison.jpg/250px-Megapixel_comparison.jpg)](/wiki/File:Megapixel_comparison.jpg)

Comparison of the level of detail between 0.3 and 24 megapixels

A **megapixel** (**MP**) is a million pixels; the term is used not only for the number of pixels in an image but also to express the number of [image sensor](/wiki/Image_sensor "Image sensor") elements of [digital cameras](/wiki/Digital_camera "Digital camera") or the number of display elements of [digital displays](/wiki/Computer_display "Computer display"). For example, a camera that makes a 2048 × 1536 pixel image (3,145,728 finished image pixels) typically uses a few extra rows and columns of sensor elements and is commonly said to have "3.2 megapixels" or "3.4 megapixels", depending on whether the number reported is the "effective" or the "total" pixel count.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

The number of pixels is sometimes quoted as the "resolution" of a photo. This measure of resolution can be calculated by multiplying the width and height of a sensor in pixels.

Digital cameras use photosensitive electronics, either [charge-coupled device](/wiki/Charge-coupled_device "Charge-coupled device") (CCD) or [complementary metal–oxide–semiconductor](/wiki/CMOS "CMOS") (CMOS) image sensors, consisting of a large number of single sensor elements, each of which records a measured intensity level. In most digital cameras, the [sensor array](/wiki/Sensor_array "Sensor array") is covered with a patterned color filter mosaic having red, green, and blue regions in the [Bayer filter](/wiki/Bayer_filter "Bayer filter") arrangement so that each sensor element can record the intensity of a single primary color of light. The camera interpolates the color information of neighboring sensor elements, through a process called [demosaicing](/wiki/Demosaicing "Demosaicing"), to create the final image. These sensor elements are often called "pixels", even though they only record one channel (only red or green or blue) of the final color image. Thus, two of the three color channels for each sensor must be interpolated and a so-called *N-megapixel* camera that produces an N-megapixel image provides only one-third of the information that an image of the same size could get from a scanner. Thus, certain color contrasts may look fuzzier than others, depending on the allocation of the primary colors (green has twice as many elements as red or blue in the Bayer arrangement).

[DxO Labs](/wiki/DxO_Labs "DxO Labs") invented the [Perceptual MegaPixel](/wiki/Perceptual_MegaPixel "Perceptual MegaPixel") (P-MPix) to measure the sharpness that a camera produces when paired to a particular lens – as opposed to the MP a manufacturer states for a camera product, which is based only on the camera's sensor. P-MPix claims to be a more accurate and relevant value for photographers to consider when weighing up camera sharpness.[[22]](#cite_note-22) As of mid-2013, the [Sigma 35 mm f/1.4 DG HSM lens](/wiki/Sigma_35_mm_f/1.4_DG_HSM_lens "Sigma 35 mm f/1.4 DG HSM lens") mounted on a [Nikon D800](/wiki/Nikon_D800 "Nikon D800") has the highest measured P-MPix. However, with a value of 23 MP, it still wipes off[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] more than one-third of the D800's 36.3 MP sensor.[[23]](#cite_note-23) In August 2019, Xiaomi released the Redmi Note 8 Pro as the world's first [smartphone](/wiki/Smartphone "Smartphone") with 64 MP camera.[[24]](#cite_note-24) On December 12, 2019, Samsung released Samsung A71 that also has a 64 MP camera.[[25]](#cite_note-25) In late 2019, Xiaomi announced the first camera phone with 108 MP 1/1.33-inch across sensor. The sensor is larger than most of [bridge camera](/wiki/List_of_bridge_cameras "List of bridge cameras") with 1/2.3-inch across sensor.[[26]](#cite_note-26)
